data.title: Hong Kong must keep cash buffer amid complicated geopolitics: Paul Chan
data.description:
Hong Kong must maintain cash reserves to cope with geopolitical shocks and support long-term growth, even with a surplus expected in the government’s operating account this year, the financial secretary has said.
Paul Chan Mo-po also said on Saturday that the city’s economy outperformed forecasts last year, lifted by strong exports to Southeast Asia and a booming financial market.
On Friday, the government announced a surplus of HK$43.9 billion (US$5.6 billion) in its operating account, which manages the daily public expenditure, for the nine months ended December 31.
But it cautioned that revenue would taper off after January, with expenditure significantly exceeding income from February to March.
While expressing understanding of different residents’ wishes for more financial incentives, Chan, who will deliver his budget speech in February, said authorities must manage their accounts cautiously.
“We should balance our current needs and middle- to long-term development, such as infrastructure construction in the Northern Metropolis,” he told a radio show, referring to the government megaproject to turn 30,000 hectares of land bordering mainland China into a technology and residential hub.
“We also need to set aside a certain amount as a buffer, as the geopolitical context now is complicated and some speculators may launch assaults against Hong Kong. In this case, we need a reserve to handle such attacks.”
Chan also expressed cautious optimism about Hong Kong’s 2026 economy after it outperformed expectations last year.
The Census and Statistics Department this week estimated that Hong Kong’s real GDP grew 3.5 per cent in 2025, surpassing an earlier forecast of 3.2 per cent.
Chan attributed the growth to robust exports to Southeast Asia, as mainland and local firms shifted production and supply chains there amid the trade war, offsetting a drop in US-bound shipments.

He added that a booming financial market and inbound tourism also contributed to the city’s economic expansion.
“But the retail and catering sectors remain under considerable pressure. In other words, our economic revival has yet to achieve balance and it will take time for strong sectors to lift others,” Chan said.
Looking ahead, Chan justified his optimism with robust forecasts for China’s economic growth and international investors’ confidence in Hong Kong and mainland markets.
He also said he expected one or two interest-rate cuts this year, with the reduction range set to be minor.
With bank deposits topping HK$19 trillion and foreign capital flowing into Hong Kong stocks, Chan called current liquidity “sufficient”.
“When the market is liquid and interest rates remain stable or even trend slightly lower, it benefits businesses and supports the capital market,” he added.
On property, Chan said the market had stabilised recently. But the supply of first-hand units would remain large over the next three to four years, with around 104,000 homes entering the market, a level that would take longer to absorb.

data.title: Chinese firms set for market growth as the global memory super cycle heats up
data.description:
In the world’s biggest wholesale electronics marketplace in the bustling Huaqiangbei district of the southern Chinese metropolis of Shenzhen, memory is the new gold.
On a Tuesday in January, a merchant surnamed Ye held up a list of prices that looked more appropriate for luxury goods than humble computer parts. A pair of 32-gigabyte, 6000-megahertz Double Data Rate 5 (DDR5) memory sticks was priced at 6,878 yuan (US$990) – having shot up nearly fivefold since September.
“In my over 10 years in this industry, this is the single most aggressive price surge I have ever seen,” said Ye, who builds computers for individuals and enterprises.
Massive global investments in artificial intelligence are driving the spike. The so-called Big Three memory makers – Samsung Electronics, SK Hynix and Micron Technology – are repurposing production lines to feed an insatiable appetite for the higher-margin memory chips used in AI data centres. As they make more of these high-bandwidth memory (HBM) and advanced dynamic random-access memory (DRAM) chips, supply has dropped for the more pedestrian chips used in personal computers, smartphones and other devices.
As painful as the skyrocketing prices are for Ye’s customers and many others, the chaos also presents a high-stakes opportunity for China’s domestic memory makers – led by ChangXin Memory Technologies (CXMT) and Yangtze Memory Technologies Corp (YMTC) – to claim a larger slice of the global market.
Memory prices have been so volatile that in Huaqiangbei, price quotes now carry expiration dates measured in hours. An engineer surnamed Li, working at a store near Ye’s, said he had simply given up on drafting written quotes for clients, as prices change two or three times a day.




Globally, memory chip prices were expected to rise by 40 per cent to 50 per cent in the first quarter of 2026, followed by a further 20 per cent jump in the second quarter, according to Counterpoint Research. Morgan Stanley analysts, meanwhile, said the steep pricing in the market would persist through 2027.
The shortage is so acute that SK Hynix reportedly sold its entire 2026 production capacity of HBM and advanced DRAM chips to major clients as early as October. Samsung Electronics, meanwhile, raised its NAND flash supply prices by more than 100 per cent in the first quarter of 2026, according to Korean media reports.
This year was shaping up as a “super cycle similar to the boom of the 1990s”, with global DRAM revenue expected to surge by 51 per cent from the previous year, according to a Bank of America report, which added that the HBM market was likely to jump 58 per cent year on year to US$54.6 billion.
Meanwhile, merchants in Shenzhen are offering domestic alternatives to budget-sensitive clients. A general manager surnamed Li at Hengxin Technologies in Huaqiangbei said around half of his clients had switched to domestic memory chips, at least partially, in their systems.
Li noted that domestic brands like CXMT were also raising prices. “Compared with products from the Big Three, domestic chips are only cheaper by about 200 to 300 yuan,” he said.
China’s largest DRAM maker, CXMT has emerged as a formidable challenger in the DRAM arena.
The company, founded in 2016, carved out a 4 per cent share of the global DRAM market last year, positioning it as the world’s fourth-largest player, although its share was well behind the Big Three, according to data from market research firm Omdia.
CXMT is moving at breakneck speed, with new products rivalling the top companies and an aggressive capital expenditure plan to ramp up production.
Its new DDR5 synchronous DRAM, unveiled in November and designed for state-of-the-art AI servers and stacks, underscored its ambition to join Samsung and SK Hynix in supplying the most advanced memory products.
The company, which currently has three 12-inch wafer fabs in Hefei, where it is based, and Beijing, is actively expanding its production capacity for advanced chips. Its monthly DRAM capacity was set to reach 330,000 wafers by the end of 2026, up from 200,000 wafers at the beginning of 2025, according to an HSBC report.
YMTC, China’s NAND flash leader, is matching that ambition. In late 2025, the Wuhan-based company launched its third factory project, with a 20.72 billion yuan price tag. The firm reportedly aims to use only domestic chipmaking equipment.
Once operational later this year, the facility would boost YMTC’s total capacity to 300,000 wafers a month, potentially raising its global NAND flash market share to 15 per cent from the current 8 per cent, according to Chinese media reports.




Chinese memory companies were becoming “a force to be reckoned with globally” as they sought a unique position in the memory landscape reshaped by AI, according to a report by Shenzhen-based consultancy Zhongtou. The rise was driven by both national strategy for tech self-sufficiency and surging domestic market demand, it said.
The expansion of the two companies is creating a windfall for the broader domestic semiconductor ecosystem. HSBC estimated that CXMT and YMTC would together make capital expenditures of more than US$28 billion in 2025 and 2026, benefiting domestic supply chain participants, especially chip equipment makers such as Naura Technology Group and Advanced Micro-Fabrication Equipment.
The ambition to capitalise on the memory super cycle is also reflected in the IPO plans of the two leading players.
CXMT in December unveiled plans to raise 29.5 billion yuan from an initial public offering (IPO) on Shanghai’s Nasdaq-style Star market. YMTC was considering a mainland IPO as early as this year with a potential valuation between 200 billion yuan and 300 billion yuan, Bloomberg reported last month, citing sources familiar with the matter.
The shortage is also driving a performance rebound for smaller domestic players.
Biwin Storage, an integrated memory firm, expected its 2025 revenue to reach between 10 billion yuan and 12 billion yuan, a year-on-year jump of between 49 per cent and 79 per cent. Its projected net profit would skyrocket by as much as 520 per cent, it said.
Montage Technology, a supplier of memory-interface chips that received approval for a Hong Kong IPO recently, expected its 2025 net profit to grow between 52 per cent and 66 per cent, while NOR flash supplier GigaDevice Semiconductor expected a 46 per cent jump in net profit.
Of course, rising memory prices are not a boon for everyone. For consumer-electronics brands, especially the country’s smartphone vendors, the situation has gone from difficult to untenable.
Xiaomi reportedly cut its 2026 shipment forecast by as much as 70 million units, while Transsion, the king of the African market, reduced its target by up to 45 million units. DreamSmart Group, owner of the Meizu brand, recently cancelled the launch of its Meizu 22 Air smartphone.
“The sharp rise in memory prices since the fourth quarter has not only impacted [production] costs, but also dealt a huge shock to our commercial plan for smartphones,” Wan Zhiqiang, the chief marketing officer at DreamSmart, said earlier this month.
Companies on the chip value chain have also signalled plans to raise prices amid the rising memory costs.

Cmsemicon, a domestic supplier of microcontrollers (MCUs), said earlier this week that it would raise prices for its MCU and NOR flash products by 15 per cent to 50 per cent, citing “a severe industry-wide supply-demand imbalance and surging costs for frames as well as packaging and testing fees”. Hunan-based Goke Microelectronics informed customers that prices for its known-good-die products – essential for advanced, tightly integrated products – would see a surge of 40 per cent to 80 per cent from January.
DRAM contract prices were projected to rise by at least 15 per cent during the first quarter, following a 40 per cent surge last quarter, which was likely to be the quarter with the largest price increase in this cycle, said Xu Jiayuan, a senior analyst with market researcher TrendForce. The price increases would slow but continue into the second half of 2026, Xu added.
“The reduction [in DRAM supply] is generally more severe for smaller brands and memory-module manufacturers,” Xu said. “Once they run out of their memory inventory in the first half of 2026, they will face greater cost pressure and the possibility of cutting production.”
On the other side of the world, the memory crunch is drawing unprecedented attention from Washington. In a sign that the sector has become a new focal point of geopolitical competition, US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick recently threatened to impose tariffs on foreign chipmakers.
Speaking at a groundbreaking ceremony for US-based Micron’s US$100 billion factory in New York, Lutnick warned that foreign memory chipmakers had two choices: “They can pay 100 per cent tariff, or they can build in America.”
The remarks, part of the broader industrial policy under the administration of US President Donald Trump, are expected to put immense pressure on South Korean giants Samsung and SK Hynix to shift more production to US soil.
For Beijing, the supply crunch and changing US policies only reinforce the urgency of its self-sufficiency drive.

Meanwhile, many traders in Huaqiangbei view the situation as a chance to seek new fortunes. Amid the supply crunch, traders are turning to used memory chips recovered from old devices. Some of them are buying used laptops from consumers for higher prices than before, just to tear them down for the valuable components inside.
“Stripping memory is stripping gold,” said Jie, a sales representative at a Huaqiangbei store.
Others see no upside in the situation.
Merchant Ye said sales had “withered significantly” because the costs were simply too high for client budgets. He was currently holding stock but felt paralysed by the market’s irrationality, he said.
“I can’t figure out exactly which part of the supply chain is stuck,” he said. “The market turnover is very low, but the prices keep skyrocketing. This is an unhealthy market, and I’m honestly pessimistic about the coming year.”

data.title: On This Day|2006 Egypt bus tragedy leaves 14 Hong Kong tourists dead – SCMP archive
data.description:
This story was published in the South China Morning Post on February 1, 2006
14 Hong Kong tour­ists die in Egypt crash
by Staff Reporters
Four­teen Hong Kong tour­ists died on Tuesday (January 31, 2006) when their speed­ing coach ran off a high­way in south­ern Egypt and rolled over.
The crash, one of the worst involving Hong Kong tour­ists, also left 30 injured, includ­ing the Egyp­tian driver.
The Jetour Hol­i­day group of 43 tour­ists, plus guides, were head­ing from the Red Sea resort of Hurghada towards the ancient city of Luxor when the crash happened about 7am (1pm Hong Kong time).
Police arres­ted the driver, Salah Mohammed Mah­mud, who was not ser­i­ously injured, and said he was likely to be charged with dan­ger­ous driv­ing.
One sur­vivor whose wife was killed said the acci­dent happened about 15 minutes into the jour­ney.
“At the time our guide was brief­ing us about the itin­er­ary,” Mr Wong, who did not give his full name, told Com­mer­cial Radio.
“I was sit­ting behind the driver and warned the guide when I saw the driver speed­ing. But it was too late, the vehicle rolled over when going past the bend.”
He said most of the pas­sen­gers were uncon­scious after the crash but he was thrown into a sand­pit and suffered only slight shoulder and facial injur­ies.
“But more than 10 people died, includ­ing my wife,” he said.

Six­teen men, 24 women and three chil­dren were on the 10-day tour, des­ig­nated KACX, that left Hong Kong on Fri­day (January 27, 2006).
The injured, some in ser­i­ous con­di­tion, were taken to the Hurghada Gen­eral Hos­pital.
“There are women and chil­dren among the vic­tims,” said Mohammed al-Sayyed, the head of Hurghada hos­pital. Jetour said some tour­ists had spinal injur­ies.
A spokes­man for the Tour­ist Police in Hurghada said the driver was exper­i­enced, “maybe the num­ber one in town”, but had been driv­ing too fast. There were no other vehicles involved and the road was in good con­di­tion.
A tour­ist police­man told the South China Morn­ing Post: “The driver was going too fast, couldn’t turn in time and drove straight off the road.”
The spokes­man said tour­ist police had cau­tioned all drivers in the area about the import­ance of driv­ing care­fully in the busy winter tour­ist sea­son fol­low­ing a sim­ilar acci­dent north of Cairo which left six Aus­trali­ans dead, also in January.
An invest­ig­a­tion into the acci­dent began within hours of Tuesday’s crash and the police had inter­viewed all the sur­viv­ors who were not ser­i­ously hurt.

The tour­ists had been stay­ing at the Hilton Hurghada Resort for the pre­vi­ous two days. A spokes­man for Hilton said the com­munity was in deep shock as news spread of the tragedy.
Jetour said inform­a­tion remained sketchy as the Hong Kong tour guide was crit­ic­ally injured in the acci­dent and the hos­pital there was too busy deal­ing with the injured. The agency has been try­ing to obtain more inform­a­tion about the crash and the vic­tims’ con­di­tions from sur­viv­ors.
Jetour oper­a­tions dir­ector Thomas Chau Wing-keung said the agency had been in touch with the rel­at­ives of most of the tour­ists and had made arrange­ments for more than 20 of them to go to Egypt on the first avail­able flight last night.
One of the rel­at­ives said at the air­port last night that he still did not know whether his elder sis­ter and her hus­band had sur­vived.
“We’re very wor­ried. We still don’t have any idea about their con­di­tion but there is noth­ing we can do,” Eric Chan Pui-kwan said.

Sec­ret­ary for Secur­ity Ambrose Lee Siu-kwong said a team of six gov­ern­ment staff – four immig­ra­tion officers, a Hos­pital Author­ity doc­tor and a nurse – had also left for Egypt and should reach Hurghada early Wednesday evening (February 1, 2006).
Chinese embassy staff in Cairo were on the way to the scene to help, he said. Xin­hua said ambas­sador Wu Sike was among the group.
“Once our med­ical team has arrived they’ll see what assist­ance they can provide,” Mr Lee said, pledging the gov­ern­ment’s fullest pos­sible assist­ance to the vic­tims.

data.title: Explainer|Blooming good: where to find cherry blossoms in Hong Kong this spring
data.description:
As spring approaches and temperatures begin to climb, Hong Kong’s cherry blossoms are preparing to burst into full bloom.
Within the next week or two, Hongkongers can enjoy this spectacle without leaving the city.
The South China Morning Post lists the best spots in the city to enjoy cherry blossoms and how to get there.
1. How to catch cherry blossoms near Hong Kong airport?
The cherry blossom garden run by the Airport Authority has expanded with 39 new trees, bringing the total to 160.
Steven Yiu Siu-chung, the authority’s executive director of airport operations, said on Thursday that while only 20 to 30 per cent of the flowers were currently blooming, the peak was expected in the week leading up to Valentine’s Day.
The garden also serves as a good spot to snap photos of both cherry blossoms and Ngong Ping 360’s cable cars.
To get there, take the MTR to Tung Chung station, where the authority has built a pavement with direct access to the garden, reducing the walking distance by 200 metres (656 feet).
Bus stops close to the garden include Aviation Fuel Tank Farm and Chek Lap Kok South Road.

2. Are there other cherry blossom hotspots?
Another popular hotpot is On King Street Park in Shek Mun, Sha Tin.
Cherry blossoms at the park have reached full bloom, according to the Leisure and Cultural Services Department’s flower appreciation website.
Some visitors are known to bring along their pet birds for photos, creating unique opportunities for snapping photos of both animals and flowers.
The park by Shing Mun River is home to over a hundred cherry blossom trees, according to the department.
It’s a 15-minute walk from Shek Mun MTR station.
If you don’t want to walk that long, take a bus and alight at Garden Vista or Ravana Garden stops – served by multiple lines linking to Hong Kong Island and beyond.
3. Where else can the blossoms be found?
Cherry blossoms can be found more or less all over Hong Kong.
Social media users have spotted the pink and white flowers in different locations, from On Lai Court in Kwun Tong to Yuen Long Park in the northwest of the city.
The flowers can also be found on campuses of some universities, including the Chinese University of Hong Kong’s New Asia College and the University of Hong Kong’s University Lodge.
4. Is the weather good to see the flowers?
You will have to wait until next week to enjoy cherry blossoms under a blue sky.
According to the Hong Kong Observatory, the weather will be mostly cloudy with occasional rain from Friday through the weekend.
The weather will improve on Monday with longer sunny spells that are expected to last until at least the next weekend.

data.title: ‘Rampant like a pandemic’: why Hongkongers struggle to get peace from junk calls
data.description:
Hongkonger Chan Shuk-wah feels exhausted by the constant ringing of her phone.
On any given weekday, nearly all of the calls lighting up her phone are from unknown numbers — as many as a dozen each day, mostly selling health checks or pushing loan applications.
The relentless calls have disrupted her professional life as a logistics company manager and as the only child of two elderly parents, both approaching 80.
“These cold calls are just rampant, like a pandemic. I’m exhausted because I don’t have the luxury of ignoring unknown numbers,” the 49-year-old said.
“Any one of them could be a legitimate business emergency or someone alerting me to my parents’ situation while I am at work.”
Chan is among the thousands of residents affected by a surge in junk calls, with 466,000 reported in 2025 alone. The figure represents a 50 per cent increase from the previous year and a 93 per cent rise from 2023, according to crowdsourced reporting platform HKJunkCall.
Among these calls, those touting loans and bank investments saw the highest growth, rising by nearly 160,000 cases, or 89 per cent, in two years. Those for healthcare check-up calls reached 58,000 last year, 10 times the figure from 2023.
Experts have argued that the Unsolicited Electronic Messages Ordinance, enacted in 2007, has become outdated due to the use of artificial intelligence to drive cold calls.
But they have also warned that a total ban remains difficult, given the need to strike a balance between protecting residents from a bombardment of calls and the legitimate marketing needs of the city’s small business sector.

The ordinance allows residents to enrol their phone numbers in “Do-Not-Call Registers” to avoid receiving faxes, messages and pre-recorded calls.
Once registered, senders are legally barred from contacting those numbers for commercial purposes after 10 working days. But the legislation leaves person-to-person interactive calls unregulated.
Ronald Pong, chairman of a committee under the Smart City Consortium, said the cold call situation had become “hopeless” because technology was moving much faster than the law.
“Many callers now use overseas servers to mimic local Hong Kong numbers, making it nearly impossible for local authorities to trace or block the source of such harassment,” he said.
Pong said mimicking local numbers allowed call centres to operate through overseas services, avoiding the cost of doing business in Hong Kong and overcoming residents’ wariness to answer foreign numbers.
“You can report as many times as you want, but a new number will eventually call you,” he said.
To combat sophisticated AI bots that exploit legal loopholes, Pong suggested using AI-filtering apps to “fight back” and screen callers before the phone even rings.
He added that Apple’s “silence unknown callers” option for iPhones provided a basic shield, while “live voicemail” offered real-time transcripts and allowed residents to instantly distinguish between a telemarketing bot and a genuine emergency without answering.
“The law simply cannot catch up with the speed of technological advancement,” Pong said.

Lawmaker Duncan Chiu, who represents the innovation and technology sector, said that while public frustration was at its peak, a complete ban on person-to-person calls could cripple local businesses reliant on direct outreach for survival.
“Some beauty parlours and fitness centres occasionally depend on cold calls to acquire new customers,” he said. “Regarding regulating telemarketing, we need to sit down and discuss it with the relevant industries.”
Chiu noted that the government was collaborating with the telecommunications sector to implement multilayered defences by utilising AI solutions to assist in blocking phone numbers that had been reported by the public or flagged as suspicious.
“The solutions offered by service providers should be effective in handling the surge in these incoming calls,” the lawmaker said.
In a reply to the South China Morning Post, the Office of the Communications Authority said some AI-driven calls were regulated under the ordinance.
“For calls that communicate with recipients solely with AI-generated voice and without any person-to-person interactive communications, or the synthesised element is not activated in response to information communicated by the caller, such a call will be subject to the regulation,” an office spokesman said.
He said the office would monitor market trends and technological advancements, such as AI in telecommunications, and assess the effectiveness of the ordinance.
The office said many service providers offered call filtering and anonymous call blocking – regardless of AI involvement – to reduce nuisance contact for those aged 60 or above, while encouraging the public to adopt filtering tools provided by their carriers to minimise suspicious calls.
The body has already mandated that service providers monitor suspicious patterns and terminate fraudulent numbers, with the initiative leading to the suspension of 1.58 million local numbers by December 2025 to combat deception, in collaboration with police, to combat phone scams.

data.title: Ant Group’s open-source push aims to move robots from lab demos to real-world work
data.description:
Chinese fintech giant Ant Group has open-sourced its first artificial intelligence models for robotics, as it steps up efforts to build machine intelligence capable of handling complex real-world tasks.
The move signals a deeper push by the Hangzhou-based Ant into embodied intelligence – AI systems designed to perceive, reason and act in physical environments rather than purely digital settings. The company is the fintech affiliate of Alibaba Group Holding, owner of the South China Morning Post.
Ant’s robotics arm, Ant Lingbo Technology, also known as Robbyant, said its latest releases include LingBot-VLA, a vision-language-action (VLA) model aimed at supporting what it described as a “universal brain” for robots, allowing more scalable and practical deployment across industries.
“For embodied intelligence to achieve large-scale adoption, we need highly capable and cost-effective foundation models that run reliably on real hardware,” said Robbyant CEO Zhu Xing. “Our goal is to accelerate the integration of AI into the physical world so it can deliver practical value sooner.”
China already leads the world in the deployment of industrial and humanoid robots.
However, many high-profile humanoids – including those developed by Unitree Robotics – that can dance and perform flips still rely on preprogrammed routines, limiting their autonomy and ability to generalise across tasks.
Chinese AI experts and investors have pointed to these limitations as a crucial obstacle to turning the new wave of robots into economically productive machines, making the development of a more powerful robotic “brain” a pressing priority for the global robotics industry.

Alongside Ant, other Chinese developers are working on embodied-AI systems, including Spirit AI and X Square Robot, the latter also backed by Alibaba.
VLA models sit at the centre of these efforts. Sometimes described as large language models adapted for robotics, they combine visual perception, language understanding and motor control to help machines perform a broad range of real-world tasks.
In a technical paper, Ant said its VLA model was tested using dual-arm robots from AgiBot and other smaller domestic start-ups – Galaxea Dynamics and AgileX Robotics – and showed stronger generalisability and training efficiency over other leading VLA models.
Robots running the model were tested across 100 tasks, including unscrewing a bottle cap, loading weights onto a dumbbell and peeling a lemon.
Data remains a key bottleneck.
Ant said it trained the model on about 20,000 hours of real-world robotics data – roughly in line with the data set used for PI*0.6, a VLA model from US start-up Physical Intelligence – but noted that a cross-platform “universal brain” spanning different robot types, from single-arm and dual-arm systems to humanoids, would require far more data.
One approach to easing this bottleneck is the use of so-called world models – simulation frameworks that allow robots to learn and practise in virtual environments, a direction highlighted by AI pioneers including Li Fei-Fei and Yann LeCun as a key next frontier for the field.
On Thursday, Ant Group also released its first world model, LingBot-World, which it said delivered capabilities comparable to the industry-leading Genie 3 system from Google DeepMind.
The move makes Ant one of the latest Chinese tech heavyweights to explore world-model approaches alongside Tencent Holdings and SenseTime.

data.title: Hang Lung flags ‘tough slog’ for property, looks to mainland malls for growth
data.description:
Hong Kong developer Hang Lung Properties is pinning its medium-term outlook on a recovery in mainland Chinese consumption, even as it warned that both the city’s and China’s property markets remain under strain.
The commercial landlord, residential developer and hotel owner said it expected a “tough slog” for the broader sector despite signs of improvement in operating conditions, particularly in the second half of the year.
Net profit attributable to shareholders fell 16 per cent to HK$1.8 billion (US$231 million) in 2025, while revenue declined 11 per cent to HK$9.95 billion, according to its latest results.
“There’s pressure across the board,” Adriel Chan, chairman of Hang Lung Properties and its parent, Hang Lung Group, said at a post-results briefing on Friday.
He added that he did not expect a rapid or broad-based rebound across property segments.
“The best hope we have is probably in mainland retail,” Chan said, citing the group’s efforts to upgrade tenant mixes and introduce new and more compelling brands across its shopping malls.
Rental revenue from its mainland mall portfolio rose 1 per cent, with occupancy climbing by two percentage points. Hang Lung operates 11 retail properties across mainland China, including projects such as Plaza 66 in Shanghai and Palace 66 in Shenyang.
The company said leasing revenue trends turned positive from May, led by assets in Shanghai, Wuxi, Dalian and Kunming.

Chan said domestic travel and Beijing’s policy push to stimulate consumption should support sales at its mainland malls, which he described as the company’s “biggest hope” for future growth.
In Hong Kong, conditions had shown early signs of stabilising since the fourth quarter, he added, though rental revenue in the city still slipped 2 per cent to HK$2.97 billion for the year.
Group CEO, Weber Lo, said performance strengthened in the latter half, supported by resilient occupancy and steady leasing income across its assets.
“We maintained high occupancy and stable leasing revenue across the portfolio, reflecting asset quality, customer focus and strong tenant relationships,” Lo said, adding that the group continued to adjust its tenant mix to match shifting consumption patterns.
Lo declined to comment on the planned closure of a four-storey outlet by Swedish fashion retailer H&M at the group’s Fashion Walk mall in Causeway Bay, where the brand has been a tenant since 2013.
Hang Lung Properties proposed a final dividend of HK$0.40 per share, payable on June 15. Its parent, Hang Lung Group, reported a 15 per cent drop in net profit attributable to shareholders to HK$1.37 billion and recommended a final dividend of HK$0.65 per share, also payable on June 15.

data.title: Hong Kong shelves cross-border levy plan, marks second policy U-turn in a day
data.description:
Hong Kong’s treasury minister has said authorities will shelve a proposed levy on private cars crossing the border into mainland China, citing feedback from lawmakers and members of the public.
Secretary for Financial Services and the Treasury Christopher Hui Ching-yu told the Legislative Council on Friday that the government had decided not to go ahead with implementing the boundary facility fee for the time being after “careful consideration”, marking the second policy U-turn of the day.
The proposed fee for private cars departing via land boundary control points was suggested in the city government’s budget for the 2025-26 financial year.
If set at HK$200 (US$26) per private car, the levy could have yielded about HK$1 billion in annual revenue for the government, according to the budget blueprint. The charge would not have affected coaches or goods vehicles.
The proposal was part of the government’s efforts to deal with its HK$87.2 billion budget deficit.
“Since we mentioned this initiative, we heard a lot of voices from many lawmakers and society. After careful consideration, we will not proceed with the levy at this stage,” Hui said at a panel meeting.
Hui’s remarks were in response to questions from lawmaker Nick Chan Hiu-fung, who told the South China Morning Post on Friday that he supported the government’s decision to drop the proposal for now.
The legislator said Hong Kong was a place where people, information and funds could flow freely across the border, adding that it would be odd to create such obstacles.
“Doing something like this suddenly could affect people from making good use of the integrated development of the Greater Bay Area,” he said. “I think placing this levy would mean that Hong Kong would lose more than it gained.”

The bay area refers to Beijing’s initiative to turn Hong Kong, Macau and nine cities in Guangdong province into an integrated economic powerhouse.
Chan added that while the government had to consider its financial situation, there was also a need to balance that against the effects such a policy would have on people’s livelihoods.
“The people who are going to the mainland aren’t just going there to shop, eat or get a massage. A lot of people are going there to do business. They are exploring potential business opportunities,” he said.
Fellow legislator Mark Chong Ho-fung said he also supported the shelving of the proposal and noted that such a measure would hinder exchanges between Hong Kong and the mainland.
He added that introducing such a levy might trigger reciprocal arrangements by cities across the border or affect how mainland visitors viewed Hong Kong.
“We have implemented a lot of measures to accelerate the contact and exchange between the mainland and Hong Kong. We have already spent a large sum of public money on this as well,” he said.
“It is meaningless to obstruct the integration of the two sides that we have been spending so much money to accomplish, just for several hundred million Hong Kong dollars.”
Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po previously rejected suggestions that the levy would hinder integration efforts, noting that the measure would only affect 15 per cent of travellers heading to the mainland.
Chan had said those who used private vehicles tended to be better off financially and it was reasonable for the government to recoup some of its costs after investing in building and maintaining border-crossing infrastructure.
Friday also saw authorities make a U-turn on the city’s seat belt requirement for bus passengers, announcing that they will repeal the law that took effect on Sunday.

data.title: 33 arrested over HK$150 million fraud involving Hong Kong tech grant schemes
data.description:
Hong Kong’s anti-corruption agency has arrested 33 people on suspicion of defrauding two government technology funding schemes of more than HK$150 million (US$19.2 million).
The Independent Commission Against Corruption (ICAC) said on Friday that it had dismantled two syndicates this month and arrested 24 men and nine women aged between 27 and 66, including a former Cyberport Management Company employee.
The two funding schemes were the Digital Transformation Support Pilot Programme (DTSPP) and the Technology Voucher Programme (TVP), overseen by the Cyberport Management Company and the Innovation and Technology Commission, respectively.
Matthew Chang Chor-ming, an ICAC principal investigator, said the two syndicates filed about 1,500 applications for both funding schemes to defraud HK$150 million in subsidies, but the watchdog managed to intercept HK$120 million.
He reiterated that the syndicates’ methods were relatively “high-end” because they not only forged documents to cheat the subsidies, but bribed various individuals to “bypass the system”.
“If the system is weak itself, it would not be necessary to take the risk and cost to bribe someone,” he said.
“The ICAC strongly condemned those who attempted to undermine the monitoring system of the technology funding programmes by bribery, defrauding subsidies paid by public funds and damaging the government’s efforts in promoting economic innovation.”
He added that authorities were working together to recover the funds defrauded by these two illegal syndicates.
Aggie Lo, an ICAC chief investigator, said nine people – six men and three women – were arrested in the DTSPP case, including owners and employees of an IT service provider and its vendors, as well as a former employee who worked at the secretariat of the programme.
Lo said the IT service provider was a registered provider at Cyberport and had submitted more than 1,000 applications on behalf of various small and medium enterprises, involving HK$50 million in subsidies.
“The IT service provider hired vendors to approach small businesses, such as snack shops, beauty parlours and wet market vendors, to help them apply for government funding,” she said.
“The syndicate claimed that the business owners could receive free digital upgrades and even cash rebates by simply signing the application forms … but most applicants had no idea what they signed up for.”
Next, the syndicate inflated the cost of very simple upgrades in the application forms, such as a basic web page or a payment QR code, to about HK$100,000 each, which was the maximum possible grant under the scheme.
They also forged documents to trick the government into believing that business owners had already paid half the cost of the expensive service.
Meanwhile, they bribed a Cyberport employee with luxury goods – such as smartphones, brand-name bags and free overseas travel – to expedite approvals and bypass the standard checks of the secretariat, leading to some of the applications being approved and subsidies being disbursed.
Following ICAC’s intervention, around 60 per cent or HK$31 million was intercepted.
Paul Lau Pak-cheong, another chief investigator, said the TVP case led to the arrest of 24 people, including 18 men and six women, aged between 30 and 66.
They are owners of IT service providers, an insurance agent, a bank manager, an accounting manager and multiple owners of shell companies who are their relatives and friends.
Lau said the syndicate initially set up pet food and jersey businesses with no actual operations to get the subsidies.
They later also recruited their relatives and friends to set up shell companies for the crime, with the help of those professionals.
The bank manager helped open bank accounts, charging HK$5,000 for each account opened, while the accounting manager and insurance agent forged transaction records and MPF contribution records, respectively.
The syndicate submitted 470 applications for the TVP for more than HK$100 million in subsidies.
More than 90 per cent of the applications, or around HK$94 million, were intercepted following ICAC’s intervention.
Cyberport said that according to the ICAC’s investigation, apart from a former employee involved in the case who had already left the company, all its other staff had diligently performed their duties.
It added that it would recover the subsidies already disbursed from the service providers who violated the regulations.

data.title: China tech giants ByteDance and Alibaba to ramp up AI infrastructure efforts
data.description:
ByteDance and Alibaba Group Holding are poised to ratchet up their artificial intelligence infrastructure initiatives, as the global AI arms race continues to intensify.
The two companies’ latest moves lend weight to Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang’s recent remarks at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, that the world was witnessing the biggest infrastructure buildout in history and that trillions of dollars of infrastructure still had to be built.
Beijing-based ByteDance, operator of TikTok and Douyin, did not only want more data centres, but also required these facilities to be used in a cheaper way, according to CEO Liang Rubo.
“Given the massive investments, efficiency is paramount. Otherwise, it amounts to significant waste,” Liang told employees on Thursday.
“We must continuously pursue cheaper computing solutions,” he said. “This involves optimising efficiency across all levels: data centres, power consumption, GPU/CPU [graphics processing unit/ central processing unit] utilisation, system architecture, algorithms and product engineering.”
ByteDance planned to spend about 100 billion yuan (US$14 billion) on AI chips from Nvidia in 2026, a hefty increase from roughly 85 billion yuan in 2025, the South China Morning Post reported last month, citing people familiar with the matter.

Alibaba, meanwhile, was considering to increase capital spending on AI infrastructure to 480 billion yuan, up from its original outlay of 380 billion yuan over the next three years, according to a report on Friday by Chinese media LatePost.
Alibaba CEO Eddie Wu Yongming in September pledged to boost the company’s AI infrastructure expenditure, but did not provide the amount of investment. Hangzhou-based Alibaba owns the SCMP.
The e-commerce giant has been investing heavily in data centres and AI systems through its Alibaba Cloud unit, as well as in semiconductors via chip design arm T-Head.
More than 100,000 units of T-Head’s Zhenwu 810E parallel processing unit, launched on Thursday, have been delivered to various clients, according to people familiar with the matter.
Chinese carmakers, including BYD and Xpeng, each ordered more than 10,000 units of the AI chip in 2025, LatePost reported.
Neither Alibaba, BYD nor Xpeng replied to a request for comment on Friday.

Tencent Holdings also pledged to step up the company’s long-term investment in AI infrastructure and talent, founder, chairman and CEO Pony Ma Huateng told employees on Monday.
In the first three quarters of 2025, Shenzhen-based Tencent’s capital spending grew 48 per cent to nearly 60 billion yuan from a year earlier. Chief financial officer John Lo said the firm increased infrastructure investment “to support [its] AI initiatives”.
ByteDance, Alibaba and Tencent were expected to receive the first batch of more than 400,000 H200 graphics processing units from Nvidia approved for import to China, according to a Reuters report.
Huang, however, said on Thursday that the licence for the H200 import was still “being finalised” by Chinese authorities.

data.title: Alibaba AI chip push hits 100,000 mark, beating local rival Cambricon: sources
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding has delivered more than 100,000 units of its most advanced artificial intelligence chip, the Zhenwu 810E, whose performance is said to be comparable to Nvidia’s H20, according to people familiar with the matter – a milestone that highlights the company’s growing role in China’s race to build domestic alternatives to US processors.
The shipments, handled by Alibaba’s semiconductor arm T-Head, have already surpassed those of domestic rival Cambricon Technologies, the sources said, declining to be named because the information is not public. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
The Zhenwu 810E is a parallel processing unit – an application-specific integrated circuit designed for both AI training and inference – and represents Alibaba’s most advanced in-house AI chip to date.
Alibaba and Cambricon did not immediately respond to requests for comment on Friday.
The move underscores how Alibaba is carving out a larger share of China’s fast-growing AI chip market, as domestic players push to reduce reliance on Nvidia amid strong demand for AI computing power and ongoing uncertainty around US export controls.
The shipment update comes days after Bloomberg reported that Alibaba was preparing a potential listing of T-Head, as investor interest in China’s semiconductor sector gathers pace.
With the Zhenwu 810E, Alibaba joins a crowded field of developers that includes tech heavyweights such as Huawei Technologies and Baidu, as well as specialist graphics processing unit designers including Moore Threads, MetaX Integrated Circuits, Biren Technology and Enflame Technology.
At least nine Chinese AI chip companies have exceeded 10,000 units in shipments or total orders – including Huawei, Baidu and Cambricon – according to Chinese financial outlet Caijing.
Cambricon has been one of the strongest performers in the domestic AI chip sector, with revenue surging more than 4,300 per cent in the first half of 2025 amid the AI investment boom, marking a record high since its 2020 listing.
Alibaba’s full-stack capabilities – spanning cloud software, AI frameworks and chip hardware – give it a competitive advantage over many domestic chip specialists, said He Hui, semiconductor research director at Omdia.
Although the Zhenwu 810E has already been widely deployed in Alibaba’s own data centres and by external clients, detailed specifications were only recently disclosed. According to T-Head, the chip supports 96 gigabytes of High Bandwidth Memory 2 Enhanced and delivers memory bandwidth of up to 700 gigabytes per second.
That places it broadly on par with Nvidia’s H20, which has faced effective sales restrictions in China since last August, though it remains behind the more powerful H200.
Earlier this week, Reuters reported that Nvidia was expected to ship an initial batch of more than 400,000 H200 chips to customers including ByteDance, Alibaba and TencentHoldings after regulatory clearance.

data.title: China’s cloud giants race to support Moltbot, the ‘AI that actually does things’Hyperscalers embrace the AI agent, which can execute tasks without step-by-step instructions, but privacy concerns loom
data.description:
China’s artificial intelligence hyperscalers have quickly added cloud support for Moltbot, formerly known as Clawdbot, an open-source AI agent that has exploded in popularity globally.
Billed as the “AI that actually does things”, Moltbot’s ability to autonomously execute tasks without the user directing each action has caused waves in the global developer community, briefly lifting shares of US web infrastructure giant Cloudflare in pre-market trading on Tuesday before they later retreated.
This week, three of China’s biggest cloud service providers – Alibaba Cloud, Tencent Cloud and ByteDance’s unit Volcano Engine – began offering access to Moltbot with support from their respective suite of models. Alibaba Cloud is the AI and cloud services unit of Alibaba Group Holding, owner of the SCMP.
While the AI agent was initially only compatible with foreign messaging apps such as Telegram and WhatsApp, Chinese tech giants have enabled interaction through their respective services, including Alibaba’s workplace collaboration platform DingTalk and Tencent Holdings’ WeCom, the work version of super app WeChat.
Smaller cloud providers have also added Moltbot, including the cloud unit of JD.com and Shanghai-based UCloud.
Created by Austrian developer Peter Steinberger, Moltbot is able to send messages, delete emails and manage files on a user’s computer autonomously. The AI agent, which users can interact with through messaging apps, has drawn attention not only for its powerful task-execution abilities but also because it was developed using another AI coding tool, without Steinberger writing the code.

However, privacy concerns have accompanied its rise. Users are required to hand over large amounts of private data and control over personal applications, prompting some to turn to separate devices – such as Apple’s Mac Mini – to protect their main devices.
As an open-source tool, Moltbot can also be integrated directly into cloud service providers’ offerings, allowing them to host data themselves.
Zhuang Shanning, a PhD student at Tsinghua University, said he used Moltbot to organise his calendar in Feishu, ByteDance’s enterprise collaboration platform, known overseas as Lark.
“While tinkering with it, I suddenly realised something: the way we interact with AI is changing,” he said. “Before, it was ‘I go out and find the tools’ myself. Now it’s ‘I say one sentence, and it gets it done’.
“Further down the line, it may be ‘I don’t need to say anything’.”
However, the tool’s heavy token usage made it expensive to run, according to Poe Zhao, a Beijing-based tech analyst and founder of Hello China Tech. In response, some Chinese cloud providers like Volcano Engine have introduced fixed-subscription options.
The global AI industry is moving towards developing AI agents capable of autonomously executing tasks on behalf of users. However, their strong autonomy as well as privacy concerns have raised questions about their regulatory future, including in China.
In December, ByteDance’s new AI-powered agentic phone faced pushback from users and domestic rivals, with several of China’s most widely used apps restricting its access.
In its announcement on Wednesday, ByteDance warned users about Moltbot’s security and privacy risks and urged them to regularly check their permission settings. “As the tool can access all device data, digital accounts and connect to the internet via multiple ports … it is recommended to deploy Moltbot on a dedicated device rather than one containing sensitive information,” the TikTok owner said.

data.title: China gives Apple ‘best iPhone quarter in history’ with 38% revenue growth
data.description:
China has emerged as the brightest spot in Apple’s quarterly earnings, as the US tech giant achieved record iPhone revenue in the world’s largest smartphone market.
Revenue in Apple’s Greater China region, which comprises mainland China, Hong Kong and Taiwan, surged 38 per cent from a year earlier in the first quarter of fiscal 2026, which ended December 27, the Cupertino-based company said on Friday.
That growth, more than double the company’s overall 16 per cent quarterly revenue increase, also marked Apple’s strongest performance in China since late 2021.
“It was the best iPhone quarter in history in Greater China,” Apple CEO Tim Cook said during an earnings call. He attributed the performance to “customer enthusiasm for the iPhone 17 line-up”.
During the quarter, traffic in Apple stores in China grew by strong double digits from a year earlier, while the company’s installed base reached an all-time high in both Greater China and mainland China, according to Cook, who described the past quarter as “terrific”.
The latest sales surge reflects how Apple’s efforts in the world’s largest smartphone market have paid off, despite sluggish consumer sentiment in the domestic market, as well as mounting pressure on the consumer-electronics supply chain from a memory-chip shortage.
Apple’s sharp recovery in China was “the most surprising result”, after the company experienced several quarters of declines due to “aggressive competition from domestic brands like Huawei”, said Francisco Jeronimo, vice-president of client devices at market researcher IDC.
Apple’s performance last quarter reflected “a robust super cycle driven by unprecedented demand for the iPhone 17 line-up, a strong resurgence in the China market, and the continued scaling of high-margin services”, Jeronimo said.
Apple shipped 16 million units to command a 21 per cent share of the mainland market in the fourth quarter, according to IDC data. For all of 2025, Apple accounted for 16.2 per cent of the market with 46.2 million units shipped, trailing closely behind Huawei Technologies, which shipped 46.7 million units for a 16.4 per cent share.
The iPhone maker could receive a further boost in China after launching a new campaign over the weekend, offering a 2,000 yuan (US$283) markdown for the iPhone Air on the Tmall e-commerce site, as well as up to a 300 yuan discount for the iPhone 16 line-up on Apple’s official website. Tmall is owned by Alibaba Group Holding, which owns the SCMP.
The iPhone continues to perform well globally. In 2025, the iPhone 16 was the world’s bestselling smartphone, with Apple models taking seven of the top 10 spots, according to Counterpoint Research.
“The iPhone 17 series achieved 16 per cent higher sales than the predecessor series during its first full quarter in the market, driven by strong initial demand in key markets such as the US, China and western Europe,” said Harshit Rastogi, an analyst at Counterpoint.

data.title: Over 110,000 get waivers within 1 month after Hong Kong public medical fee reform
data.description:
More than 110,000 patients secured fee waivers within a month since Hong Kong’s public medical fee reform took effect this year, a jump from 14,000 for a whole year previously, the health minister said while defending the new regime.
Secretary for Health Lo Chung-mau said on Friday that among the more than 110,000 people with approved waivers, about 53,000 had already received certificates for fee reduction.
The roughly 60,000 patients remaining who had failed to provide income and asset proof were granted three-month conditional waivers, he said, adding that they must still submit the relevant documents and that authorities would conduct random checks to prevent abuse of medical resources.
“It has been less than a month since the reform was implemented, but the number of people receiving the waiver has increased from only 14,000 a year in the past to more than 110,000 people,” he said at a meeting of the Legislative Council’s panel on health services.
“More people with real financial needs have been added.”
Some lawmakers questioned the effectiveness and impact of the public medical fee reform during the meeting.
Legislator Chan Hok-fung said the increased costs at public hospitals under the new arrangement had put more financial burden on those who were not eligible for a fee waiver. He said some private medical services had also raised charges in the wake of the reform in the public sector.
“I think the purpose of increasing the fees at public hospitals is to divert some services to private medical institutions,” he said.
“But in addition to the fee increase, authorities seem to have failed to control the increase in fees charged by private institutions.
“As a result, I have heard that some private doctors have begun to increase fees.”

In response, Lo said: “We currently do not have any data indicating that private medical charges have changed due to our public fee adjustments.”
He added that authorities would monitor the situation.
The new public medical fee regime was implemented on January 1 to better support patients in need, with relaxed income and asset limits for a waiver that made an extra 1.1 million Hongkongers eligible for aid.
Under the new arrangement, the income threshold for a fee waiver was relaxed from 75 per cent of the median monthly household income to 100 per cent. The asset limit was revised to match the levels for applying for public rental housing.
Under the new fee structure, each visit to an accident and emergency department at a public hospital costs HK$400 (US$51) for less urgent cases, up from HK$180, while consultation fees at family clinics have risen from HK$50 to HK$150 per session.

data.title: Alibaba’s T-Head unit unveils details of AI chip designed to rival Nvidia’s GPUsThe launch of the Zhenwu 810E marks the latest initiative by China’s Big Tech firms to develop a domestic alternative to
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding’s semiconductor design arm, T-Head, on Thursday unveiled details of its advanced artificial intelligence chip with capabilities said to be on par with Nvidia’s H20, days after the unit was reportedly being prepared for a potential spin-off and public listing.
According to information on T-Head’s website, the Zhenwu 810E was a “fully self-developed” parallel processing unit (PPU), an application-specific integrated circuit designed for both AI training and inference. Alibaba owns the SCMP.
Developed by T-Head to handle the massive data throughput required for generative AI systems, the Zhenwu 810E supports 96 gigabytes of High Bandwidth Memory 2 Enhanced – a 3D-stacked synchronous dynamic random-access memory standard designed for AI, machine learning and high-performance computing.
A single card with the T-Head PPU handles a bandwidth of 700 gigabytes per second, which made it on par with Nvidia’s H20 – a graphics processing unit (GPU) tailored by Nvidia specifically for China to comply with US export restrictions.
China last year summoned Nvidia over alleged “back door safety risks” in the H20, which signalled a de facto ban on its sales to local firms.
T-Head’s launch of the Zhenwu 810E marked the latest initiative by China’s Big Tech firms, including Huawei Technologies and Baidu, to develop a domestic alternative to Nvidia’s GPUs and bolster the country’s technology self-reliance efforts.
Before Washington and Beijing stepped up their regulatory scrutiny of the firm last year, Nvidia said it had 95 per cent of the advanced chip market in China.
Alibaba’s Hong Kong-listed stock was unchanged at HK$173.30 on Thursday.

While details of the Zhenwu 810E were made public this week for the first time, the advanced processor had already been deployed in multiple 10,000-chip computing clusters of Alibaba Cloud – the AI and cloud services unit of Hangzhou-based Alibaba – as well as large external clients, according to T-Head.
The T-Head-designed chip was first unveiled in a report by state broadcaster China Central Television that aired on September 16, which simply identified it as PPU. It showed Premier Li Qiang being briefed by China Unicom on the use of mainland-developed chips in the telecommunications network operator’s infrastructure.
The report showed T-Head’s PPU being compared with Nvidia’s H20 and A800 GPUs in a performance benchmark at a data centre operated by China Unicom in northwestern Qinghai province.
Founded in 2018, T-Head said its self-developed Inter-Chip Network technology provided high bandwidth and low latency training and inference for large language models. The Zhenwu 810E was also suitable for autonomous driving applications, it added.
The Zhenwu 810E, however, still lagged behind the performance of the H200, which delivers roughly six times the performance of the H20. Nvidia’s second most powerful GPU was expected to start shipping to Chinese clients from this week.
An initial H200 shipment of more than 400,000 units was expected to be delivered to ByteDance, Alibaba and Tencent Holdings, according to a Reuters report.

data.title: Hong Kong economy grows 3.5% in 2025, beating earlier forecasts
data.description:
Hong Kong’s economy expanded by 3.5 per cent in 2025, marking the city’s third consecutive year of growth and surpassing an earlier forecast of 3.2 per cent.
Advance estimates released by the Census and Statistics Department on Friday also showed gross domestic product (GDP) in the fourth quarter increased by 3.8 per cent compared with the previous year.
Hong Kong’s GDP expanded by at least 3 per cent in each of the first three quarters of 2025, rising 3 per cent year on year in the first quarter, 3.1 per cent in the second and 3.8 per cent in the third.
The economy expanded by 3.2 per cent and 2.5 per cent year on year in 2023 and 2024, respectively. The full-year GDP figure also exceeded the government’s earlier forecast of a 3.2 per cent increase.
Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po had previously raised Hong Kong’s GDP growth forecast from between 2 and 3 per cent to 3.2 per cent, attributing the stronger projection to robust exports, resilient investment and a buoyant asset market.
For 2025 as a whole, private consumption expenditure increased by 1.6 per cent compared with a year earlier, following a 0.7 per cent decline in 2024.
Total exports of goods and services rose by 12 per cent and 12.6 per cent, respectively, for the whole of the year, compared with 4.7 per cent and 5.1 per cent growth in 2024.
Chan expressed cautious optimism about the economic outlook for 2026 after attending the World Economic Forum in Davos last week, citing Hong Kong’s stock market performance and the US’ trade surplus with the city in services.
He pledged to maintain financial stability and diversify the range of companies listed on the city’s stock exchange.

data.title: China’s humanoid robot sales to double this year as costs fall: Morgan Stanley
data.description:
Morgan Stanley expects China’s humanoid robot sales to grow 133 per cent to 28,000 units this year, while noting that falling production costs will gradually make owning robots more accessible.
The US investment bank revised its forecast from the 14,000 units previously on the back of the rapid growth of the industry in the world’s second largest economy.
The cost of materials to produce robots in China, home to most of the industry’s supply chain, was set to drop 16 per cent this year, the bank said. The consulting firm Bain & Co, meanwhile, expects parts prices to decrease by roughly 70 per cent globally by 2035.
This would lead to lower prices for humanoid robots – to around US$21,000 by 2050 from US$50,000 in 2024 – in middle and low-income countries, including China, according to Morgan Stanley.
In rich nations like the US, the average price would decrease to US$75,000 from US$200,000 in the same period, it added.
The US investment bank predicted humanoid installations would eventually become more pervasive. By 2036, some 25.4 million human-shaped machines would be in use, accounting for 2 per cent of the market that includes robotic arms and wheeled robots.
That proportion would reach 13 per cent by 2040 and 42 per cent by 2044.


By 2050, around 1 billion humanoid robots would be adopted, with more than half in upper middle-income countries, including China, according to Morgan Stanley.
China already leads the world in terms of adoption, accounting for more than 80 per cent of humanoid robot installations in 2025, according to market intelligence provider Counterpoint Research.
Bain said that for now robots would be extensively deployed in the automotive, mining and construction sectors, while the consumer market would take off by 2035, with a focus on social, education and domestic purposes.
Non-humanoid robots would execute highly repetitive workflows, leaving humanoids to do other general tasks, it added.
Morgan Stanley also said that it believed non-humanoid robots would “see faster near-term commercialisation, with better immediate return on investment and stability”.
The bank said it had upgraded China’s market sales volume “based on the latest 2025 numbers and current progress”.
Last week, Unitree Robotics, one of the industry leaders, said its shipment of humanoids surpassed 5,500 units in 2025, while actual orders were higher.
AgiBot, another giant, expected its 2026 shipment to reach “tens of thousands”, Chinese media Jiemian reported, citing company executive Zhu Jie.
Morgan Stanley expects China’s sales of humanoid robots to rise exponentially – to 262,000 units by 2030 and 2.6 million by 2035 from around 12,000 last year.
Aggressive promotions have already started. A few robot makers announced partnerships with the Spring Festival Gala, one of the world’s most watched television shows, which will be aired by the state broadcaster China Central Television on February 16.
Apart from a deal with Unitree and Magiclab for the gala, many other firms including ByteDance’s cloud unit Volcano Engine, e-bike maker Shouqu and robot cleaning brands Dreame and Mova will be highlighted during the event.
The show has evolved from a cultural fixture into a high-stakes proving ground for market influence. Last year, Unitree made headlines at the gala when its six humanoid robots performed a synchronised folk dance.
Separately, Tesla CEO Elon Musk said in an earnings call on Wednesday that “by far the biggest competition for humanoid robots will be from China”, which “is incredibly good at scaling, manufacturing” and “quite good at AI”.
Additional reporting by Xiaofei Xu
